\begin{document}

\title{A Data-Driven Capacity-Reservation System for\\
Cloud Cost Optimization under Intermittent Demand Surges}

\author{%
\IEEEauthorblockN{First Author\IEEEauthorrefmark{1},
Second Author\IEEEauthorrefmark{1}, and
Third Author\IEEEauthorrefmark{1}}
\IEEEauthorblockA{\IEEEauthorrefmark{1}Department of Scientific Computing, Faculty of Computer and Information Sciences\\
\textit{Affiliation Placeholder}, City, Country\\
Email: \{author1, author2, author3\}@example.edu}%
}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Enterprises increasingly run on public clouds, yet a large fraction of cloud
spend is wasted because demand is a low, stationary \emph{base} load punctuated
by intermittent random \emph{surges}, while pricing offers a discounted but
committed \emph{reserved} tariff alongside an expensive but elastic
\emph{on-demand} tariff. The operations-management literature characterizes the
cost-optimal response as a capacity-reservation policy---a newsvendor sizing of
\emph{base} and \emph{supplementary} reserved contracts with the residual met by
on-demand spillover, governed by a two-threshold $(s,S)$ rule---but stops at
closed-form policies for stylized demand. We ask whether that policy can be
turned into a deployable, data-driven system. We present Smart Cloud Optimizer,
which (i) forecasts per-account cost and utilization with five time-series models
selected by walk-forward cross-validation, (ii) sizes reserved capacity with a
mixed-integer program whose right-hand sides are an empirical critical fractile
(the $95$th percentile of utilization inflated by a $1.3\times$ headroom), and
(iii) applies eight rule-based actions for reserved/on-demand contract choice and
service-specific waste. On a \nDays-day, multi-service workload, exponential
smoothing attains \bestMape\% mean absolute percentage error at a $30$-day
horizon; the optimizer produces \numRecs{} recommendations worth
\$\totalSavings/month, a \savingsPctBill\% reduction of the \$\totalBill/month
bill. We make explicit where the deployed system departs from the optimal
policy. The evaluation workload is synthetic and modeled on public cloud traces,
not a live account; we report this as a threat to validity.
\end{abstract}

\begin{IEEEkeywords}
cloud cost optimization, capacity reservation, newsvendor, reserved instances,
time-series forecasting, mixed-integer programming, FinOps.
\end{IEEEkeywords}

% =====================================================================
\section{Introduction}
% =====================================================================
Public-cloud adoption continues to accelerate, but cost governance has not kept
pace: industry surveys consistently report that roughly a quarter to a third of
cloud spend is wasted on idle or over-provisioned resources~\cite{flexera2024soc}.
The root cause is a mismatch between how demand behaves and how capacity is
priced. Demand for a typical workload is a \emph{base} load---low, stationary,
and predictable---punctuated by intermittent \emph{surges} driven by promotions,
launches, batch jobs, or seasonal events~\cite{fox2009berkeley,shen2015bitbrains}.
Pricing, meanwhile, is two-sided: a \emph{reserved} (committed) tariff that is
cheap per unit but is paid whether used or not, and an \emph{on-demand} tariff
that is elastic with zero commitment but costs substantially more per unit.
Choosing how much capacity to reserve versus leave to on-demand is therefore a
classic trade-off between commitment risk and elasticity premium.

Chen, Lei, and Moinzadeh~\cite{chen2024capacity} formalize this trade-off as a
capacity-reservation problem. A firm holds \emph{base contracts} sized to the
stationary base demand and \emph{supplementary contracts} for surges; demand
beyond reserved capacity spills to on-demand. They show that the cost-optimal
capacities follow a \emph{newsvendor critical fractile}, and that the optimal
management of supplementary contracts (purchase, renewal, cancellation,
expiration) is a two-threshold $(s,S)$ policy. This is an elegant and complete
\emph{characterization}, but it presumes the demand distributions are known and
deterministic in structure, and it produces a policy rather than an operational
pipeline that an engineering team can run against a real account.

A parallel systems literature attacks the same waste empirically---forecasting
utilization and reacting with autoscaling or instance
acquisition~\cite{chaisiri2012provisioning,wang2014optimal,hosseini2020switch,kirchoff2024provisioning,agullo2025forecasting}---but
rarely connects its heuristics back to the cost-optimal reservation policy, and
seldom reports end-to-end dollar savings with a reproducible methodology. The two
bodies of work are complementary: one supplies the economic optimum, the other
supplies the data and tooling.

\textbf{This paper bridges the two.} We present \emph{Smart Cloud Optimizer}, a
system that operationalizes the base/supplementary/on-demand reservation
framework of~\cite{chen2024capacity} on real account telemetry. We adopt that
paper's vocabulary and sizing principle---base and supplementary reserved
capacity, on-demand spillover, the newsvendor fractile, and the two-threshold
$(s,S)$ view---as the \emph{lens} for a deployable pipeline built from
forecasting, mixed-integer optimization, and rule-based actions. Our
contributions are:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{A practical realization of the capacity-reservation policy.} We
show that a deployed right-sizing rule---reserve to the $95$th percentile of
forecasted utilization with a $1.3\times$ headroom---is an \emph{empirical
critical fractile}, and we relate the implied service level to the cost-optimal
newsvendor fractile (Section~\ref{sec:model}).
\item \textbf{An end-to-end system} that forecasts cost/utilization (five models,
walk-forward selection), sizes capacity with a mixed-integer program, and chooses
reserved-vs-on-demand contracts and service-level waste removal with eight rules
(Section~\ref{sec:system}).
\item \textbf{A reproducible numerical study} on a \nDays-day, multi-service
workload: exponential smoothing reaches \bestMape\% MAPE at a $30$-day horizon,
and the optimizer yields \numRecs{} recommendations worth \$\totalSavings/month
($\savingsPctBill\%$ of the bill). Every number is regenerated by a single script
from the database (Section~\ref{sec:eval}).
\item \textbf{An honest account of the gap} between the deployed system and the
optimal policy, and of the synthetic nature of the workload
(Section~\ref{sec:threats}).
\end{enumerate}

The remainder follows the arc of~\cite{chen2024capacity}:
Section~\ref{sec:related} positions the work, Section~\ref{sec:model} states the
reservation model and newsvendor/$(s,S)$ policy, Section~\ref{sec:system}
describes the realizing system, Section~\ref{sec:eval} reports the numerical
study, Section~\ref{sec:threats} discusses threats to validity, and
Section~\ref{sec:conclusion} concludes.

% =====================================================================
\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related}
% =====================================================================
We organize related work into the three streams of~\cite{chen2024capacity},
extended with the forecasting stream we draw on.

\textbf{(1) Capacity reservation under random demand and surges.}
The reservation/option-contract view of capacity has a long history in
operations: backup and reservation contracts trade commitment for
flexibility~\cite{eppen1997backup,erkoc2005reservation}, and planning for random
demand surges combines reactive capacity with safety
stock~\cite{huang2016surges}. State-modulated (base-vs-surge) demand is modeled
classically via Markov-modulated processes in inventory and
queueing~\cite{songzipkin1993,fischer1993mmpp,heffes1986mmpp}, and the optimal
single-period commitment is the newsvendor critical
fractile~\cite{arrow1951inventory}. Our modeled reference~\cite{chen2024capacity}
specializes this to cloud reserved/on-demand pricing with cancellation and a
secondary marketplace; Furman and Diamant~\cite{furman2025capacity} study a
complementary provider-side capacity problem under periodic time-varying demand.
We borrow the \emph{policy} from this stream and ask how to realize it.

\textbf{(2) Cloud cost optimization and instance acquisition.}
On the cloud-user side, stochastic programming chooses reservation levels under
demand and price uncertainty~\cite{chaisiri2012provisioning}; online and
brokerage algorithms acquire reserved/on-demand instances over
time~\cite{wang2014optimal,wang2014dynamic}; scheduling and instance switching
cut rental cost~\cite{hosseini2020switch}; and platform-side work studies
overcommitment~\cite{cohen2019overcommitment}, slack
capacity~\cite{nunez2021slack}, bundled procurement~\cite{arbabian2021capacity},
preemptible discounts~\cite{chen2021discount,chen2019pricing}, and the reserved
marketplace~\cite{ambati2020reservations}. These works are largely algorithmic or
analytical; we contribute a working pipeline that emits dollar-quantified
recommendations.

\textbf{(3) Forecasting-driven provisioning.}
Time-series forecasting underpins proactive
provisioning~\cite{kirchoff2024provisioning,agullo2025forecasting,pintye2024autoscaling}.
We use established models---exponential smoothing/Holt--Winters~\cite{winters1960holtwinters,hyndman2021fpp},
automatic ARIMA~\cite{hyndman2008forecast}, and Prophet~\cite{taylor2018prophet}
via statsmodels~\cite{seabold2010statsmodels}---together with streaming anomaly
detection in the spirit of the Numenta Anomaly
Benchmark~\cite{ahmad2017nab,lavin2015nab}. To our knowledge, none of these works
ties the forecast to the \emph{newsvendor} reservation optimum and reports the
realized account-level saving, which is our integrative aim.

% =====================================================================
\section{Reservation Model and Policy}\label{sec:model}
% =====================================================================
We restate the model of~\cite{chen2024capacity} in the notation of
Table~\ref{tab:notation}, then derive the sizing principle our system
approximates.

\begin{table}[t]
\caption{Model notation (after~\cite{chen2024capacity}).}
\label{tab:notation}
\centering
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabular}{@{}c l@{}}
\toprule
Symbol & Meaning \\
\midrule
$w_0$ & on-demand cost rate per unit capacity per period \\
$w_T$ & reserved cost rate, $w_T<w_0$ (commitment of length $T$) \\
$\gamma$ & reserved/on-demand ratio $w_T/w_0\in(0,1)$ \\
$d_0,d_1$ & base / surge demand per period, $d_1$ dominates $d_0$ \\
$F_0,F_1$ & CDFs of base and surge demand \\
$t_d,t_s$ & surge duration / inter-surge duration \\
$K_0$ & base capacity (reserved indefinitely) \\
$K_1$ & total capacity during a surge; $K_1-K_0$ supplementary \\
$\pos{x}$ & $\max(x,0)$, the on-demand spillover operator \\
$\alpha$ & fraction of reserved value recovered on cancellation \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\textbf{Cost structure.}
A firm serves a state-modulated demand: a stationary base state (demand $d_0$,
fraction $\tfrac{t_s}{t_s+t_d}$ of time) and a surge state (demand $d_1$, fraction
$\tfrac{t_d}{t_s+t_d}$). Holding base capacity $K_0$ and surge capacity $K_1$, the
long-run average cost rate is reserved cost plus on-demand spillover
priced at $w_0$:
\begin{align}
\mathrm{AC}(K_0,K_1) &= \underbrace{w_T K_0}_{\text{base contracts}}
+ \underbrace{w_T (K_1-K_0)\,\tfrac{t_d}{t_s+t_d}}_{\text{supplementary contracts}}
\nonumber\\
&\;+ w_0\!\left[\tfrac{t_s}{t_s+t_d}\E\!\pos{d_0-K_0}
+ \tfrac{t_d}{t_s+t_d}\E\!\pos{d_1-K_1}\right]\!.
\label{eq:ac}
\end{align}
The first two terms are committed reserved spend; the bracket is the expected
\emph{on-demand spillover}, the demand that exceeds reserved capacity.

\textbf{Newsvendor optimality.}
Minimizing~\eqref{eq:ac} yields a critical-fractile condition. In the
single-capacity case ($K_0=K_1=K$) the optimum $K^\star$
solves~\cite[Prop.~1]{chen2024capacity}
\begin{equation}
F_0(K^\star) + \tfrac{t_d}{t_s}F_1(K^\star)
= \frac{w_0-w_T}{w_0}\left(1+\tfrac{t_d}{t_s}\right),
\label{eq:newsvendor}
\end{equation}
and in the dual-capacity case the optimal base and surge capacities are newsvendor
quantiles of $F_0$ and $F_1$,
\begin{equation}
K_0^\star = F_0^{-1}\!\big(q_0\big),\qquad
K_1^\star = F_1^{-1}\!\big(q_1\big),
\label{eq:fractiles}
\end{equation}
with fractiles $q_0,q_1$ that depend on $w_T/w_0$, the cancellation recovery
$\alpha$, and the duration ratio~\cite[Thm.~1]{chen2024capacity}. Managing the
supplementary contracts over time is a two-threshold $(s,S)$ policy on the
elapsed cycle time: cancel below a lower threshold, hold to expiration above an
upper threshold, and otherwise renew.

\begin{remark}[Service level reading]
Ignoring the surge term, \eqref{eq:newsvendor} reduces to the textbook newsvendor
$F(K^\star)=1-w_T/w_0=1-\gamma$: the optimal reserved capacity covers the
$(1-\gamma)$ quantile of demand. Cheaper reserved capacity (small $\gamma$) means
reserve more; reserved capacity that is barely discounted (large $\gamma$) means
reserve little and let demand spill to on-demand.
\end{remark}

\textbf{From policy to an empirical fractile.}
Our system does not know $F_0,F_1$ analytically; it estimates them from telemetry.
For a resource $i$ with observed per-period utilization $u_i$, the deployed
right-sizing rule sizes the required capacity to
\begin{equation}
r_i = q_{0.95}(u_i)\cdot V_i \cdot h,\qquad h=1.3,
\label{eq:p95}
\end{equation}
where $q_{0.95}(u_i)$ is the $95$th percentile of utilization, $V_i$ the current
resource size, and $h$ a headroom factor. Equation~\eqref{eq:p95} is exactly a
\emph{critical-fractile} capacity choice with service level
$q\!\approx\!0.95$. Reading it through the remark, a $0.95$ service level is
cost-optimal only when $\gamma\!\approx\!0.05$ (near-free reserved capacity);
for realistic reserved discounts ($\gamma\in[0.6,0.9]$) the cost-optimal fractile
is $0.10$--$0.40$. The deployed heuristic is therefore deliberately
\emph{conservative}: it favors avoiding performance-impacting under-provisioning
(spillover) over extracting the last dollar, and the $1.3\times$ headroom adds
further margin. We make this gap explicit rather than claim the heuristic is
cost-optimal; closing it (tuning $q$ to the measured $\gamma$) is future work.

% =====================================================================
\section{System: Forecasting + MILP + Rules}\label{sec:system}
% =====================================================================

\begin{figure*}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.96\textwidth]{fig_architecture.pdf}
\caption{System architecture. Telemetry is collected per AWS service, stored in
SQLite, and consumed by three engines---forecasting, the reservation optimizer
(MILP $+$ rules), and an LLM advisor---and surfaced in a dashboard. The optimizer
realizes the newsvendor/$(s,S)$ reservation policy of Section~\ref{sec:model}.}
\label{fig:arch}
\end{figure*}

Fig.~\ref{fig:arch} shows the pipeline. A collection layer gathers inventory,
CloudWatch metrics, and Cost Explorer data for ten AWS services (EC2/EBS, RDS,
S3, Lambda, DynamoDB, ElastiCache, ECS, ELB, NAT) into a per-account SQLite store;
three engines then consume it.

\subsection{Forecasting the demand distribution}
The forecasting engine implements five models behind one interface emitting
$[\textit{date},\textit{forecast},\textit{lower},\textit{upper}]$: a naive
last-value baseline, a seasonal-naive (weekly) baseline, additive
Holt--Winters exponential smoothing
(ETS)~\cite{winters1960holtwinters,hyndman2021fpp}, automatic seasonal ARIMA
(SARIMAX)~\cite{hyndman2008forecast}, and Prophet~\cite{taylor2018prophet}. Models
are compared by \emph{walk-forward cross-validation}: an expanding training window
of at least \cvInitial{} days, a step of \cvStep{} days, and a forecast horizon
$h$; mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) is averaged over folds. The selected
model provides the empirical base distribution $\hat F_0$ and prediction
intervals; points exceeding $\mu+2\sigma$ are flagged as surges (the realization
of the state-1 / $d_1$ regime), in the spirit of streaming anomaly
detection~\cite{ahmad2017nab}.

\subsection{Sizing capacity with a MILP}
For compute (EC2, RDS) the optimizer solves a mixed-integer program with
CBC via PuLP~\cite{mitchell2011pulp}. Let $\mathcal{I}$ be the in-scope
instances, $\mathcal{C}$ the catalog of candidate types with price $p_c$, vCPUs
$v_c$, and memory $m_c$, and $x_{i,c}\in\{0,1\}$ assign type $c$ to instance $i$:
\begin{align}
\min_{x}\;& \sum_{i\in\mathcal{I}}\sum_{c\in\mathcal{C}} p_c\,x_{i,c}
\label{eq:milp}\\
\text{s.t.}\;& \textstyle\sum_{c} x_{i,c}=1 && \forall i\in\mathcal{I},\nonumber\\
& \textstyle\sum_{c} v_c\,x_{i,c}\ge r_i^{\mathrm{cpu}} && \forall i,\nonumber\\
& \textstyle\sum_{c} m_c\,x_{i,c}\ge r_i^{\mathrm{mem}} && \forall i,\nonumber\\
& \textstyle\sum_{i}\sum_{c} p_c\,x_{i,c}\le B,\;\; x_{i,c}\in\{0,1\},\nonumber
\end{align}
where the capacity requirements $r_i^{\mathrm{cpu}},r_i^{\mathrm{mem}}$ are the
empirical fractiles of~\eqref{eq:p95} and $B$ is a budget cap. The objective picks
the cheapest catalog type whose vCPU and memory cover the fractile-sized load:
this is the computational analogue of choosing $K_0$ in~\eqref{eq:fractiles}, with
the residual tail $\pos{d-r_i}$ left to on-demand spillover.

\subsection{Choosing reserved vs.\ on-demand, and removing waste}
Eight rule-based actions complete the policy (Algorithm~\ref{alg:pipe}). Two
\emph{pricing} rules realize the base-contract decision: an on-demand resource
with sufficient usage history is switched to a one-year reservation when the
reserved rate beats on-demand---i.e., committing the always-on base capacity while
leaving variable load on-demand. The remaining six target service-level waste:
EBS \texttt{gp2}$\rightarrow$\texttt{gp3} migration, deletion of unattached/idle
volumes and idle load balancers, NAT-gateway$\rightarrow$VPC-endpoint
replacement, S3 intelligent-tiering, DynamoDB capacity-mode switching, and Lambda
memory right-sizing. Each action emits a per-resource saving
$\Delta_i=c_i^{\text{cur}}-c_i^{\text{new}}$; the engine de-duplicates by
$(\text{resource},\text{action})$ and persists the recommendations.

\begin{algorithm}[t]
\caption{Reservation-policy realization for one account}
\label{alg:pipe}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require account telemetry $\mathcal{D}$, catalog $\mathcal{C}$, budget $B$
\State $\hat F \gets$ \textsc{SelectModel}($\mathcal{D}$) \Comment{walk-forward CV}
\State flag surges where cost $>\mu+2\sigma$ \Comment{state-1 demand}
\State $R \gets \emptyset$
\For{each compute service $s\in\{$EC2, RDS$\}$}
  \State $r_i \gets q_{0.95}(u_i)\,V_i\,h$ for $i\in\mathcal{I}_s$ \Comment{Eq.~\eqref{eq:p95}}
  \State $x^\star \gets$ solve MILP~\eqref{eq:milp} with $(r_i,B)$
  \State add right-size recs where $x^\star_i\neq$ current and cheaper
\EndFor
\For{each rule $\rho$ in the eight actions}
  \State $R \gets R \cup \rho(\mathcal{D})$ \Comment{reserved switch / waste removal}
\EndFor
\State de-duplicate $R$ by (resource, action); keep max saving
\State \Return $R$, $\sum_{r\in R}\Delta_r$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

A cold-start advisor (a large-language-model prompt over a nine-question
requirements form) covers accounts with no usage history; it is orthogonal to the
reservation policy and we do not evaluate its dollar impact here.

% =====================================================================
\section{Numerical Study}\label{sec:eval}
% =====================================================================

\subsection{Setup}
Following the structure of~\cite[Sec.~7]{chen2024capacity}, we first fix the
workload, then evaluate forecasting and optimization. The workload is a single
representative account observed for \nDays{} days (\dataStart{} to \dataEnd),
spanning \numServicesBilled{} billed service categories and \numResources{}
resources (including \numEctwo{} EC2 instances). Daily cost averages
\$\meanDaily{} (standard deviation \$\stdDaily, range \$\minDaily--\$\maxDaily),
totaling \$\annualTotal{} over the year. Defining a \emph{demand surge} as a day
exceeding $\mu+2\sigma=\$\surgeThresh$ isolates \numSurges{} pronounced spikes
(Fig.~\ref{fig:daily}); the largest reaches $z=\maxZ\sigma$ above the mean. These
are precisely the intermittent state-1 events of the model, and they recur
irregularly across the year---exactly the regime the reservation policy targets.
EC2 dominates the bill (Fig.~\ref{fig:share}), so compute right-sizing and
reservation carry the largest savings potential.

\begin{figure*}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.96\textwidth]{fig_daily_surges.pdf}
\caption{Daily cost over \nDays{} days. The dotted line is the surge threshold
$\mu+2\sigma=\$\surgeThresh$; \numSurges{} days (markers) exceed it. Base demand
is stationary around \$\meanDaily/day; surges are intermittent and large.}
\label{fig:daily}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Forecasting performance}
Table~\ref{tab:mape} and Fig.~\ref{fig:mape} report walk-forward MAPE by model and
horizon. Exponential smoothing (ETS) is the most robust model and the best at the
deployment-relevant $30$-day horizon (\mapeEts\%), with the seasonal-naive
baseline competitive at short horizons. Prophet (\mapeProphet\% at $30$ days)
trails ETS here---its yearly-seasonality components overfit a single year of
data---and the naive baseline (\mapeNaive\%) confirms the series is non-trivial.
We therefore select ETS. Fig.~\ref{fig:holdout} shows its $30$-day holdout
forecast with a $95\%$ interval: ETS tracks the base load at \holdoutMape\% MAPE
with \holdoutCov\% empirical interval coverage, but the lone in-window surge
(2025-06-03) pierces the upper band. This is the model's---and the policy's---core
message: \emph{surges are not forecastable from the base process}; they are the
spillover the reservation policy intentionally routes to on-demand rather than
provisioning reserved capacity against.

\begin{table}[t]
\caption{Walk-forward MAPE (\%) by model and horizon (days). Lower is better;
best per column in bold. Expanding window, initial \cvInitial{} days, step
\cvStep{} days.}
\label{tab:mape}
\centering
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabular}{@{}l r r r r r@{}}
\toprule
Model & $h{=}7$ & $h{=}14$ & $h{=}30$ & $h{=}60$ & $h{=}90$ \\
\midrule
ETS (selected)   & 11.28 & 11.24 & \textbf{11.20} & \textbf{11.39} & \textbf{12.15} \\
SeasonalNaive    & \textbf{10.18} & \textbf{10.84} & 12.28 & 12.66 & 12.91 \\
Prophet          & 14.41 & 20.47 & 20.93 & 19.70 & 18.08 \\
Naive            & 25.16 & 27.90 & 27.26 & 27.87 & 27.38 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{fig_mape_horizon.pdf}
\caption{Walk-forward MAPE vs.\ horizon. ETS is most accurate and stable; both
beat the naive baseline. SARIMAX is excluded for runtime (minutes per fold).}
\label{fig:mape}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{fig_forecast_holdout.pdf}
\caption{ETS $30$-day holdout forecast with $95\%$ interval. The base load is
tracked at \holdoutMape\% MAPE; the single surge on 2025-06-03 exceeds the band
(on-demand spillover).}
\label{fig:holdout}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Optimization savings}
The optimizer produces \numRecs{} recommendations (plus \numAiRecs{} advisory
items) totaling \$\totalSavings/month. Against the \$\totalBill/month bill this is
a \savingsPctBill\% reduction, taking monthly spend to \$1599.08; on the
\$\flaggedCost{} of resources actually flagged, the reduction is
\savingsPctFlagged\%. Fig.~\ref{fig:savings} breaks the savings down by action.
The two compute levers dominate: MILP right-sizing of over-provisioned databases
(\$\savRightsize, \cntRightsize{} recs) and the reserved/pricing
switch on always-on instances (\$\savPricing, \cntPricing{} recs) together account
for \$571.23, i.e.\ $96.8\%$ of the total. The six waste-removal rules
(delete-unused, NAT, EBS, S3, Lambda) contribute the long tail. This distribution
matches the model's intuition: the largest savings come from \emph{committing base
capacity correctly} (reservation and right-sizing), not from micro-optimizations.
By confidence, \cntHigh{} high-confidence recommendations carry \$\savHigh{} and
\cntMed{} medium-confidence ones \$\savMed.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{fig_savings_breakdown.pdf}
\caption{Recommended monthly savings by optimization action (USD; recommendation
count in parentheses). Right-sizing and the reserved/pricing switch dominate.}
\label{fig:savings}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.86\columnwidth]{fig_service_share.pdf}
\caption{Share of the monthly bill by service category (Cost Explorer buckets;
non-itemized services fold into ``Other''). EC2 dominates, so compute reservation
carries the largest savings.}
\label{fig:share}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Policy comparison and sensitivity}
The headline comparison mirrors the reference's cancellation-vs-trade study but
on realized cost: the unoptimized (largely on-demand) baseline of
\$\totalBill/month versus \$1599.08/month under the forecast-driven
reservation/right-sizing policy---a \savingsPctBill\% saving without any change to
the workload. The sensitivity that matters for the policy is the service level.
As Section~\ref{sec:model} shows, the cost-optimal reserved fractile is
$1-\gamma$; Table~\ref{tab:fractile} contrasts it with the deployed $q\!=\!0.95$
heuristic across reserved-discount levels analogous to the
$\gamma\in\{0.85,0.90,0.95\}$ sweep of~\cite{chen2024capacity}. The deployed
system is uniformly more conservative, which protects against spillover during
surges at the cost of some reserved over-commitment---an explicit, tunable
trade-off rather than an oversight.

\begin{table}[t]
\caption{Cost-optimal reserved service level $1-\gamma$ vs.\ the deployed
$q{=}0.95$ heuristic, by reserved/on-demand ratio $\gamma=w_T/w_0$.}
\label{tab:fractile}
\centering
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabular}{@{}l c c c c@{}}
\toprule
$\gamma=w_T/w_0$ & 0.60 & 0.70 & 0.85 & 0.90 \\
\midrule
optimal fractile $1-\gamma$ & 0.40 & 0.30 & 0.15 & 0.10 \\
deployed fractile $q$       & 0.95 & 0.95 & 0.95 & 0.95 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

% =====================================================================
\section{Threats to Validity}\label{sec:threats}
% =====================================================================
\textbf{Workload provenance.} The evaluation uses a synthetic, single-account
dataset that ships with the system; its base/surge structure and per-service
utilization are modeled on publicly documented cloud traces---the Bitbrains
GWA-T-12 business-critical workload~\cite{shen2015bitbrains,iosup2008gwa} and the
Numenta Anomaly Benchmark~\cite{ahmad2017nab,lavin2015nab}---but it is \emph{not}
a live AWS account and no trace-conversion generator is bundled with the artifact.
Absolute dollar figures are therefore illustrative of the method, not a measured
saving on production infrastructure; the \emph{methodology} (Section~\ref{sec:eval})
is what transfers.

\textbf{Policy gap.} The deployed system realizes the \emph{static} newsvendor
sizing, not the full $(s,S)$ dynamics of~\cite{chen2024capacity}: the reserved
switch is a per-resource, all-or-nothing one-year commitment rather than a
fractional base/supplementary split with cancellation and renewal. Right-sizing
(on-demand-priced) and reservation are computed independently and can co-apply to
one resource, so the summed saving is an upper estimate of what is jointly
realizable. Implementing the two-threshold cancellation/renewal policy is the
natural next step.

\textbf{Forecasting and detection.} MAPE excludes zero-cost days and is sensitive
to the surge days; the $\mu+2\sigma$ surge rule is a global threshold rather than
the rolling detector used elsewhere in the codebase, and SARIMAX is omitted from
the comparison for runtime. The prediction-interval semantics differ across models
(ETS uses $\pm1.96\sigma$ of residuals).

\textbf{Generality.} Results are from one account and one year; multi-account and
multi-year validation, and a live-account deployment, remain open.

% =====================================================================
\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}
% =====================================================================
We turned the capacity-reservation policy of Chen, Lei, and
Moinzadeh~\cite{chen2024capacity}---base and supplementary reserved contracts,
on-demand spillover, a newsvendor critical fractile, and a two-threshold $(s,S)$
rule---into a deployable, data-driven system. Forecasting estimates the demand
distribution, a mixed-integer program sizes reserved capacity at an empirical
critical fractile, and eight rules choose reserved-vs-on-demand contracts and
remove service-level waste. On a \nDays-day multi-service workload, exponential
smoothing forecasts the base load at \bestMape\% MAPE while surges escape the
interval as designed, and the optimizer recovers \$\totalSavings/month
(\savingsPctBill\% of the bill), concentrated---as the theory predicts---in
correctly committing base capacity. We were explicit about the gap to the optimal
$(s,S)$ policy and about the synthetic workload. Closing that gap by implementing
the cancellation/renewal dynamics, tuning the service level $q$ to the measured
reserved discount $\gamma$, and validating on live multi-account data are our next
steps.
